topics: Surdas's poem about Krishna and butter
Krishna's clever arguments to his mother Yashoda
subject: Hindi

sections.heading: 🧙 The Quest Begins — Who is Surdas?
sections.body: Our wizard of words today is Surdas — a great poet who lived about 500 years ago! He was born in the 15th century and spent most of his life near Mathura and Govardhan, singing beautiful songs about Lord Krishna. Even though Surdas could not see, his imagination was so powerful — like a magical spell — that he wrote the most beautiful poems about Krishna's childhood. His poems are written in Braj Bhasha and people still love and sing them all over India today!
keyPoints: Surdas lived in the 15th century and wrote in Braj Bhasha language
He could not see, but his imagination was his superpower — like a wizard's spell
His poems about Krishna's childhood are famous and sung across India

sections.heading: 🐄 The Adventure — Krishna's Big Excuse!
sections.body: Imagine your little brother eating all the mithai and then telling your mother 'I didn't eat it!' — that is exactly what Krishna does here! In this poem, Krishna says to his mother Yashoda: 'Maiya, I did NOT eat the butter!' He says he was busy since morning — sent to the forest of Madhuban to look after the cows. He says he roamed around with his flute (bansuri) for four pahars (about 12 hours!) and only came home in the evening. He gives so many reasons — just like a clever little detective trying to win his quest!
keyPoints: Krishna says he was sent to graze cows in Madhuban forest since morning
He claims he was busy all day — four pahars — and came home only at sunset
One pahars equals three hours, so four pahars means twelve hours of work!

sections.heading: 🐲 Fighting the Dragon — Krishna's Cleverest Arguments!
sections.body: Krishna does not stop at one excuse — he fights the dragon of blame with many clever arguments! He says: 'I am a small child, my arms are tiny — how can I even reach the hanging pot (chheeka)? It is kept so high up!' Then he says his friends, the Gwal-baal (cowherd boys), are his enemies — they grabbed his face and forcefully smeared butter on his mouth to get him in trouble! Just like in cricket, when a player says 'it was not my fault, the ball hit the bat by itself!' Krishna blames everyone else.
keyPoints: Krishna says his arms are too small to reach the chheeka — the hanging pot where butter is kept
He blames his cowherd friends, saying they smeared butter on his face to get him in trouble
A chheeka is like a hanging basket used in Indian homes to keep food away from cats and dogs

sections.heading: ❤️ The Spell Breaks — Yashoda's Love Wins!
sections.body: After all these arguments, what does Krishna say to his mother? He says: 'You are too innocent, Maata — you believe whatever others say! There must be something in your heart that makes you treat me like a stranger!' Now Yashoda knows Krishna ate the butter — of course she does! But she cannot be angry for long. Surdas tells us that Yashoda smiled and pulled Krishna close to her heart and hugged him tightly. Just like when your mother knows you ate the last biscuit but still hugs you — love always wins the quest!
keyPoints: Krishna accuses Yashoda of believing others more than him and calls her too innocent (bholi)
Yashoda sees through all his excuses but melts with love and hugs Krishna
Surdas ends the poem beautifully — showing that a mother's love is the greatest power

sections.heading: ✨ Magic of the Poem — Rhyme and Special Words!
sections.body: This poem has a special magic called 'tuk' — which means rhyme! Just like in Diwali songs where the last words of each line sound similar, in this poem words like 'pathayo' and 'aayo' rhyme with each other. Every line ends with a similar sound — like a musical spell! Also, Surdas follows an old tradition of putting his own name in the last line of his poem. This is called 'chhaap' — his signature in the poem, just like signing your name on your drawing!
keyPoints: Tuk means rhyme — the last words of each line sound similar, making the poem musical
Surdas signs his name in the last line — this is his poet's signature called chhaap
Some words in this Braj Bhasha poem are slightly different from today's Hindi — like 'paache' instead of 'peechhe'

summary: In this quest, we explored Surdas's famous poem where little Krishna uses every clever argument he can think of to convince his mother Yashoda that he did NOT eat the butter! He says he was busy with cows all day, that his arms are too small to reach the hanging pot, and that his friends framed him. Yashoda knows the truth but her love is so strong that she simply smiles and hugs him. Surdas — the great blind poet of the 15th century — captures this beautiful moment of a mother's love and a child's innocence in his magical Braj Bhasha poem. The poem teaches us about rhyme (tuk), old Hindi words, the measurement of time using 'pahars', and most importantly — that a mother's love is the most powerful spell in the world!
